note_data.title: Artificial intelligence
keyPoints: Artificial Intelligence is covered in Paper 3 Advanced Theory under the Systems Software section
The course develops computational thinking skills including abstraction, decomposition and algorithmic thinking
Programming paradigms are essential ways of thinking about or approaching problems in computer science
Communication involves understanding rules and methods for data transfer between devices and components
Computer architecture and hardware form a key conceptual foundation of the subject
The course encourages learners to be confident using a range of technology and programming paradigms

definitions.definition: A set of fundamental skills that help produce a solution to a problem, using skills such as abstraction, decomposition and algorithmic thinking
A way of thinking about or approaching problems, with different programming styles suited to unique functions, tools and specific situations
The ability to transfer data from one device or component to another and understanding of the rules and methods used in this data transfer

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give characteristics and features. Use bullet points for clarity.
Give similarities AND differences between two AI concepts

modelAnswer.question: Explain the difference between supervised and unsupervised machine learning. [4 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: Supervised learning uses labelled training data where the correct answers are provided to the algorithm. The algorithm learns from input-output pairs to make predictions on new data. For example, email spam detection uses emails already classified as spam or not spam. Unsupervised learning uses unlabelled data with no target outputs provided. The algorithm finds hidden patterns or structures in the data without knowing the correct answers. For example, customer segmentation groups customers based on purchasing behaviour without predefined categories.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Supervised learning uses labelled/training data
Mark 2: Algorithm learns from input-output pairs/correct answers provided
Mark 3: Unsupervised learning uses unlabelled data/no target outputs
Mark 4: Finds hidden patterns without knowing correct answers

commonMistakes: Not understanding that AI is part of the Systems Software section in Paper 3
Failing to apply computational thinking skills systematically when approaching problems
Not recognizing the importance of selecting appropriate programming paradigms for specific situations
examinerTips: Always check the latest syllabus available from the public website for current content requirements
Focus on developing transferable skills including computational thinking and problem solving as these are highly valued
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Neural network diagram showing input layer (data inputs), hidden layer(s) with weighted connections between nodes, and output layer producing final results. Each connection has a weight value, and nodes apply activation functions to process signals.
pastPaperRefs: M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to explain three ways artificial intelligence is used in modern vehicles and discuss one advantage and one disadvantage of each use
O/N 2022 P2 Q5c (4 marks) — candidates had to describe how machine learning differs from traditional programming and give two examples of machine learning applications
M/J 2022 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — multiple choice question testing knowledge of expert systems and their use in medical diagnosis
O/N 2021 P2 Q3b (5 marks) — students were required to explain how neural networks process information and describe their use in image recognition software

workedExample.question: Explain how an expert system works and describe its use in medical diagnosis. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define what an expert system is - a computer program that mimics human expertise in a specific field
Step 2: Explain the knowledge base component - contains facts, rules and expertise from human specialists
Step 3: Describe the inference engine - processes user input against the knowledge base to reach conclusions
Step 4: Explain the user interface - allows doctors to input symptoms and receive diagnostic suggestions
Step 5: Give specific medical example - MYCIN system diagnoses bacterial infections and suggests treatments
Step 6: Mention advantages - consistent diagnosis, available 24/7, reduces human error
workedExample.answer: An expert system is a computer program that mimics the decision-making ability of a human expert in a specific domain. It consists of a knowledge base containing facts, rules and expertise gathered from human specialists, and an inference engine that processes user queries against this knowledge to reach logical conclusions. In medical diagnosis, doctors input patient symptoms through a user interface, and the system suggests possible diagnoses and treatments. For example, the MYCIN system diagnoses bacterial infections by analyzing symptoms and medical history. Expert systems provide consistent diagnosis, are available 24/7, and help reduce human diagnostic errors.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for definition of expert system, 1 mark each for knowledge base and inference engine explanation, 2 marks for medical application description, 1 mark for specific advantage

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused artificial intelligence with simple automation' — always distinguish between rule-based programming and true AI that can learn and adapt

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently gave vague examples like robots without explaining the AI component' — always specify how machine learning, neural networks or expert systems are actually used in your examples
Examiner Report 2021: 'Candidates often failed to explain both advantages AND disadvantages when asked' — ensure you provide balanced arguments and don't just focus on benefits of AI systems
note_data.keyDiagram: Draw a flowchart showing the components of an expert system: User Interface (top) connects bidirectionally to Inference Engine (center), which connects to Knowledge Base (left containing 'Facts, Rules, Expertise') and Working Memory (right containing 'Current session data'). Show arrows indicating data flow: user input goes down, system queries go between inference engine and knowledge base, and conclusions flow back up to user interface.
note_data.quickRecap: Artificial Intelligence is studied as part of Paper 3 Advanced Theory under Systems Software. The course emphasizes computational thinking as a fundamental skill set involving abstraction, decomposition and algorithmic thinking. Students develop understanding of programming paradigms and communication methods while being encouraged to use technology confidently and responsibly.
